keyAchievements.title: 이스트먼 코닥 설립
keyAchievements.description: 이스트먼 코닥이라는 회사를 설립했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 롤 필름 출시 및 사진 촬영 대중화 기여
keyAchievements.description: 롤 필름을 출시하며 사진 촬영을 주류로 이끄는 데 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 영화 필름 발명의 토대 마련
keyAchievements.description: 롤 필름은 1888년 영화 필름의 발명의 토대가 되었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 조지 이스트먼 박물관 국립역사기념물 지정
keyAchievements.description: 조지 이스트먼 박물관은 미국 국립역사기념물로 지정되었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 할리우드 명예의 거리 투 스타
keyAchievements.description: 할리우드 명예의 거리의 투 스타로 대표되는 유일한 인물입니다.

faq.question: 조지 이스트먼은 어떤 회사와 관련이 있나요?
faq.answer: 조지 이스트먼은 이스트먼 코닥이라는 회사를 설립했습니다.

faq.question: 조지 이스트먼의 주요 업적은 무엇인가요?
faq.answer: 롤 필름을 출시하여 사진 촬영을 주류로 이끄는 데 기여했으며, 롤 필름은 1888년 영화 필름 발명의 토대가 되었습니다.

faq.question: 조지 이스트먼 박물관은 어떤 의미를 가지나요?
faq.answer: 조지 이스트먼 박물관은 미국 국립역사기념물로 지정되었습니다.

timeline.event: 엘리샤 그레이브스 오티스 출생 (8월 3일)
19세에 자신이 살던 집을 떠나 뉴욕으로 이주했다.
안전 장치가 달린 엘리베이터를 발명했다.
뉴욕에서 오티스 엘리베이터를 설립했다.
뉴욕 만국박람회에서 안전 장치가 달린 엘리베이터를 공개 시연했다.
오티스 엘리베이터가 뉴욕에서 승객을 위한 최초의 엘리베이터를 설치했다.
엘리베이터에 안정성에 관련된 특허를 받았으나 곧 사망했다 (4월 8일).

keyAchievements.title: 현대식 엘리베이터 발명
keyAchievements.description: 안전 장치가 달린 엘리베이터를 발명하여 현대식 엘리베이터의 시초를 마련했다.

keyAchievements.title: 오티스 엘리베이터 설립
keyAchievements.description: 1853년 뉴욕에서 오티스 엘리베이터를 설립했다.

keyAchievements.title: 안전 엘리베이터 공개 시연
keyAchievements.description: 1854년 뉴욕 만국박람회에서 올라가던 엘리베이터의 밧줄을 끊어 안전 장치의 작동을 공개적으로 시연했다.

keyAchievements.title: 최초의 승객용 엘리베이터 설치
keyAchievements.description: 오티스 엘리베이터를 통해 1857년 뉴욕에 승객을 위한 최초의 엘리베이터를 설치했다.

faq.question: 엘리샤 오티스는 무엇으로 유명한가요?
faq.answer: 그는 현대식 엘리베이터의 발명자이자 오티스 엘리베이터의 설립자입니다.

faq.question: 엘리샤 오티스는 언제 안전 엘리베이터를 발명했나요?
faq.answer: 그는 1851년에 안전 장치가 달린 엘리베이터를 발명했습니다.

faq.question: 오티스 엘리베이터는 언제 설립되었나요?
faq.answer: 오티스 엘리베이터는 1853년 뉴욕에서 설립되었습니다.

faq.question: 엘리샤 오티스는 어떻게 안전 엘리베이터를 시연했나요?
faq.answer: 1854년 뉴욕 만국박람회에서 올라가던 엘리베이터의 밧줄을 끊어 안전 장치의 작동을 보여주었습니다.

timeline.event: 윤사웅, 최천구 등과 함께 중국에 파견되어 천문기기의 모양을 배움
천문기기 제작 공을 인정받아 면천되었고, 종5품 상의원 별좌에 임명됨
정5품 행사직으로 승진하고 첫 물시계인 갱점지기 제작을 명받음
정4품 호군 관직을 더함
수력 자동 물시계 자격루를 완성함
이천이 총책임자였던 금속활자 갑인자 주조에 참여함
천상시계 옥루를 완성하여 흠경각에 설치함

keyAchievements.title: 자격루
keyAchievements.description: 자동으로 시간을 알리는 수력 물시계인 자격루를 완성했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 옥루
keyAchievements.description: 해와 달의 움직임을 모형으로 보여주며 시간, 계절, 천체의 움직임을 관측할 수 있는 천상시계 옥루를 제작했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 천문 기구
keyAchievements.description: 대간의, 소간의, 현주일구, 천평일구, 정남일구, 앙부일구, 일성정시의, 규표 등 다양한 천문 관측 기구들을 제작했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 갑인자
keyAchievements.description: 구리로 만든 금속활자인 갑인자 주조에 참여하여 인쇄 기술 발전에 기여했습니다.

faq.question: 장영실의 출생년도는 언제인가요?
faq.answer: 장영실의 출생년도는 명확히 기록되어 있지 않으나, 1423년 면천 당시 나이가 약 34세였다는 기록으로 미루어 1389년에서 1390년 사이에 태어난 것으로 추측됩니다.

faq.question: 장영실의 신분은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 장영실은 본래 동래현의 관노였으나, 뛰어난 기술력을 인정받아 태종과 세종에 의해 발탁되었습니다.

faq.question: 장영실은 어떤 이유로 관직에서 물러났나요?
faq.answer: 세종대왕의 어가가 부서지는 사건으로 인해 임금에 대한 불경죄로 간주되어 삭탈관직을 구형받았고, 이후 역사에서 자취를 감추었습니다.

faq.question: 장영실의 가족 관계는 어떻게 되나요?
faq.answer: 장영실의 아버지는 원나라 유민으로 소주·항주 출신이며, 어머니는 조선 동래현의 기생이었습니다. 여동생이 한 명 있었고, 사촌 여동생은 천문학자 김담에게 시집갔다고 합니다.

jang-yeong-sil.missingDataNote: 출생년도와 사망년도가 명확히 기록되어 있지 않으며, 관직에서 물러난 이후의 행적에 대한 기록이 없습니다.

광명중학교 졸업

치안유지법 위반 혐의로 일본 경찰에 체포
교토 지방 재판소에서 2년형 선고 및 후쿠오카 형무소 수감
후쿠오카 형무소에서 옥사
경향신문에 유작 처음 소개 및 추도회 거행
유고시집 《하늘과 바람과 별과 시》 초간본 임시 발간
유고시집 《하늘과 바람과 별과 시》 정식 간행
연세대에 유작 〈서시〉가 새겨진 ‘윤동주 시비’ 건립
윤동주문학상 제정 및 수상자 선정 시작
건국공로훈장 독립장 추서
용정 중학교에 〈서시〉 시비 건립
일본 도시샤 대학에 〈서시〉 시비 건립
한국예술평론가협의회 선정 20세기를 빛낸 한국의 예술인

keyAchievements.title: 독립운동가, 시인, 작가 활동
keyAchievements.description: 일제강점기 조선의 독립운동가, 시인, 작가로 활동했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 문예지 《새 명동》 발간
keyAchievements.description: 명동소학교 재학 시절 문예지 《새 명동》을 발간하여 문학 활동을 시작했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 문단 등단
keyAchievements.description: 연희전문학교 재학 중 《소년》지에 시를 발표하며 정식으로 문단에 등단했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 유고시집 《하늘과 바람과 별과 시》 출간
keyAchievements.description: 사후에 그의 대부분의 작품이 실린 유고시집 《하늘과 바람과 별과 시》가 출간되어 큰 반향을 일으켰습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 양심적 지식인으로 인정
keyAchievements.description: 일제강점기 후반의 양심적 지식인으로 인정받았으며, 그의 시는 일제 비판과 자기성찰을 담고 있습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 민족적 저항시인 평가
keyAchievements.description: 민족적 저항시인이자 강인한 의지와 부드러운 서정을 지닌 시인으로 평가됩니다.

keyAchievements.title: 한국인이 가장 좋아하는 시인 선정
keyAchievements.description: 1991년 한국인이 가장 좋아하는 시인으로 선정되었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 건국공로훈장 독립장 추서
keyAchievements.description: 1990년 대한민국 정부로부터 건국공로훈장 독립장이 추서되어 그의 공훈을 기렸습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 윤동주문학상 제정
keyAchievements.description: 그의 시정신을 계승하기 위해 1985년 한국문인협회에 의해 윤동주문학상이 제정되었습니다.

faq.question: 윤동주는 언제, 어디서 태어났나요?
faq.answer: 윤동주는 1917년 12월 30일 북간도 명동촌에서 태어났습니다.

faq.question: 윤동주는 어떤 학교를 졸업했나요?
faq.answer: 명동소학교와 길림광명중학교를 졸업했으며, 연희전문학교를 등업사했습니다.

faq.question: 윤동주의 대표적인 시집은 무엇인가요?
faq.answer: 그의 대부분의 작품은 사후에 출간된 유고시집 《하늘과 바람과 별과 시》에 실려 있습니다.

faq.question: 윤동주는 왜 일본에서 체포되었나요?
faq.answer: 1943년 7월 14일 치안유지법에 따른 사상범으로 일본 경찰에 체포되어 후쿠오카 형무소에 수감되었습니다.

faq.question: 윤동주는 언제 사망했나요?
faq.answer: 윤동주는 1945년 2월 16일 후쿠오카 형무소에서 옥사했습니다.

faq.question: 윤동주가 창씨개명을 했다는 것이 사실인가요?
faq.answer: 1941년 말 '히라누마'로 창씨개명했으며, 이는 일본 유학을 위한 어쩔 수 없는 선택이었다는 견해가 유력합니다.

faq.question: 윤동주는 어떤 평가를 받나요?
faq.answer: 그는 민족적 저항시인이자 강인한 의지와 부드러운 서정을 지닌 시인으로 평가되며, 그의 시는 식민지 지식인의 고뇌와 자기성찰을 담고 있습니다.

timeline.event: 아테네의 행정 구역 콜로노스에서 부유한 기사의 아들로 태어남.
살라미스 해전을 기리는 연회에서 선창 소년으로 뽑혀 노래함.
29세에 디오니소스제의 비극 경연에서 선배인 아이스킬로스를 물리쳐 명성을 떨침.
델로스 동맹의 10명으로 구성된 통솔자에 선출됨.
정치 생활에 들어가 요직을 여러 번 지냈으며, 해군 제독으로 활약함.
사망.

keyAchievements.title: 그리스 3대 비극 시인
keyAchievements.description: 아이스킬로스, 에우리피데스와 함께 고대 그리스의 3대 비극 시인으로 꼽힙니다.

keyAchievements.title: 비극 경연 우승
keyAchievements.description: 디오니소스제의 비극 경연에서 24회 1등 우승을 차지하며 비극예술의 완성자로 불렸습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 비극 예술 혁신
keyAchievements.description: 3부극을 폐지하고 합창 대원을 늘렸으며, 세 배우를 등용하고 소도구와 배경화를 채용하여 극의 발전에 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 정치 및 군사 활동
keyAchievements.description: 델로스 동맹의 통솔자와 해군 제독을 지내는 등 펠로폰네소스전쟁 전후로 정치 생활에 참여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 현존하는 작품
keyAchievements.description: 《트라키스 여인들》, 《아이아스》, 《안티고네》, 《오이디푸스 왕》, 《엘렉트라》, 《필록테테스》, 《콜로노스의 오이디푸스》 등 7편의 비극 작품이 현존합니다.

keyAchievements.title: 사후 영웅 칭호
keyAchievements.description: 사망 후 아테네 시민으로부터 '덱시온'이라는 영웅 칭호를 받았습니다.

faq.question: 소포클레스는 언제 태어나고 사망했나요?
faq.answer: 소포클레스는 기원전 497년에 태어나 기원전 406년에 사망했습니다.

faq.question: 소포클레스는 어떤 인물로 평가받나요?
faq.answer: 고대 그리스 아테네의 비극 시인으로, 아이스킬로스, 에우리피데스와 함께 그리스의 3대 비극 시인으로 꼽힙니다.

faq.question: 소포클레스는 비극 예술에 어떤 기여를 했나요?
faq.answer: 비극예술의 완성자로 불리며, 3부극을 폐지하고 합창 대원을 늘렸으며, 세 배우를 등용하는 등 극의 발전에 기여했습니다.

faq.question: 소포클레스의 대표적인 현존 작품은 무엇인가요?
faq.answer: 《트라키스 여인들》, 《아이아스》, 《안티고네》, 《오이디푸스 왕》, 《엘렉트라》, 《필록테테스》, 《콜로노스의 오이디푸스》 등 7편이 있습니다.

faq.question: 소포클레스는 정치 활동도 했나요?
faq.answer: 펠로폰네소스전쟁 전후로 정치 생활에 참여하여 델로스 동맹의 통솔자 및 해군 제독으로 활약했습니다.

sophocles.missingDataNote: 16살 되던 해에 발생한 사건의 정확한 연도는 원문에 명시되어 있지 않습니다.

timeline.event: 에우클레이데스 출생
프톨레마이오스 1세 소테르 재위 기간 동안 알렉산드리아에서 활동
에우클레이데스 사망

keyAchievements.title: 《원론》
keyAchievements.description: 총 13권으로 기하학과 정수론을 다루며, 일정한 공리에서 결과를 이끌어내는 논리적인 전개로 수학사에서 가장 영향력 있는 저술 중 하나이다.

keyAchievements.title: 《주어진 값》
keyAchievements.description: 기하학을 다룬 저서이다.

keyAchievements.title: 《현상》
keyAchievements.description: 구면 기하학을 다룬 저서이다.

keyAchievements.title: 《광학》
keyAchievements.description: 광학을 다루는 그리스 최고(最古)의 문헌이다.

keyAchievements.title: 《도형의 분할에 대하여》
keyAchievements.description: 주어진 도형을 주어진 비로 분할하는 문제를 다루며, 아랍어 번역으로 부분만 현존한다.

keyAchievements.title: 《거울 광학》
keyAchievements.description: 거울을 사용한 기하학 문제를 다룬다.

faq.question: 에우클레이데스에게 수학의 실용성에 대해 질문한 제자에 대한 일화가 있나요?
faq.answer: 에우클레이데스의 강연 도중 한 제자가 수학의 실용성에 대해 묻자, 에우클레이데스는 그 제자에게 동전 세 닢을 주고 강의실 밖으로 쫓아내라고 말했다는 유명한 일화가 전해진다.

euclid.missingDataNote: 에우클레이데스의 삶에 관해 알려진 바가 많지 않으며, 그에 대한 언급은 그가 살았던 세기 후에 쓰여진 것이 대부분이다. 《거울 광학》은 후대의 작품으로 추측되며, 여러 저서가 손실되어 짧은 인용만 전해진다.

timeline.event: Born as Mdungazwe, son of the Gaza Empire's king, Mzila.
Ascends to the throne after his father's death, defeating his brothers in a succession war and taking the name Gungunhana.
Moves his capital from the north to Manjacaze in the south, closer to the center of Portuguese influence.
Portuguese forces under Joaquim Mouzinho de Albuquerque declare war. Gungunhana is captured at the sacred village of Chaimite in December.
After being paraded through Lisbon, he is exiled to the Azores islands, a Portuguese archipelago in the Atlantic.
Dies in exile on Terceira Island in the Azores.
His remains are repatriated to Mozambique and reburied with the honors of a national hero.

keyAchievements.title: Consolidation of the Gaza Empire
keyAchievements.description: After a succession crisis, he successfully unified the empire and asserted his authority over rival factions.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Maneuvering
keyAchievements.description: He skillfully attempted to play British and Portuguese colonial interests against each other to preserve his empire's independence.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Resistance
keyAchievements.description: His determined, though ultimately unsuccessful, war against Portuguese colonization made him a national hero and an icon of anti-colonial struggle in Mozambique.

faq.question: Why is he called the 'Lion of Gaza'?
faq.answer: This name refers to his fierce reputation as a warrior and his determined resistance against the Portuguese, symbolizing the strength and pride of his Gaza Empire.

faq.question: Did Gungunhana have any allies?
faq.answer: He attempted to form alliances, notably with the British South Africa Company led by Cecil Rhodes, to counter the Portuguese. However, these diplomatic efforts ultimately failed to provide the military support he needed.

faq.question: What happened to his body after he died?
faq.answer: He was buried in the Azores. In 1985, his remains were repatriated to Mozambique, where he was reburied with the honors of a national hero, solidifying his legacy.

timeline.event: Born as the daughter of Usman dan Fodio, the founder of the Sokoto Caliphate.
Lives through the Fulani War, which leads to the establishment of the Sokoto Caliphate.
Establishes the 'jaji' (or Yar Taru) network of itinerant female teachers.
Her brother, Muhammed Bello, becomes the second Caliph, and she serves as one of his most trusted advisors.
Dies in Sokoto, leaving a legacy of over 60 written works and a revolutionary educational system.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Jaji (Yar Taru) Network
keyAchievements.description: She established a system of itinerant female teachers to educate women in rural communities across the Caliphate.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet and Scholar
keyAchievements.description: She authored over 60 works in Arabic, Fula, and Hausa, documenting history, law, and theology.

keyAchievements.title: Advisor to the Caliph
keyAchievements.description: She served as a trusted advisor to her brother, Caliph Muhammed Bello, influencing state policy on education and social welfare.

faq.question: What does 'Nana' mean in her name?
faq.answer: It is a title of respect, similar to 'Princess' or 'Lady,' not her given first name. Her full name was Asma'u bint Usman dan Fodio.

faq.question: What languages did Nana Asma'u speak?
faq.answer: She was a polyglot, fluent in at least four languages: Arabic, Fula (Fulfulde), Hausa, and Tamacheq (the language of the Tuareg).

faq.question: Is her legacy still relevant today?
faq.answer: Yes, her work is a major historical precedent for Muslim feminism and female education in Africa, and her educational movement continues to inspire modern initiatives.

timeline.event: Born in Egypt, though her parentage is not definitively known.
Marries Pharaoh Amenhotep IV, who later changes his name to Akhenaten.
Akhenaten initiates his religious revolution. The royal family moves to the new capital, Akhetaten (modern Amarna).
Nefertiti disappears from surviving historical records. Her fate is unknown.
Akhenaten dies, leading to the eventual restoration of the old gods under Tutankhamun.
The famous painted limestone bust of Nefertiti is discovered in Amarna by German archaeologist Ludwig Borchardt.

keyAchievements.title: Religious Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Co-led the radical shift from traditional Egyptian polytheism to the monotheistic worship of the sun-disk, Aten.

keyAchievements.title: Co-Ruler of Egypt
keyAchievements.description: Wielded immense power, often depicted in poses of pharaonic authority, suggesting a status nearly equal to her husband, Akhenaten.

keyAchievements.title: Icon of Amarna Art
keyAchievements.description: Became the central subject of a new, naturalistic art style that depicted the royal family with unprecedented intimacy and realism.

faq.question: What does your name mean?
faq.answer: My name, Nefertiti, means 'the beautiful one has come' in the ancient Egyptian language.

faq.question: Why did you and Akhenaten worship only one god?
faq.answer: We believed the Aten, the sun-disk, was the universal creator and sustainer of all life, not just in Egypt but across the world. We sought to replace the complex and, in our view, corrupt old pantheon with this single, pure source of light and truth.

faq.question: What happened to you?
faq.answer: My fate after the 12th year of my husband's reign is a mystery lost to the sands of time. Some believe I died, others that I fell from grace, and some even speculate I ruled after him as a pharaoh. The records are silent, but my legacy endures.

timeline.event: Traditional date for the founding of Carthage by Dido, as calculated from ancient sources.
Traditional date of Dido's death, according to the historian Timaeus.
The Roman poet Virgil writes the 'Aeneid', the epic poem that contains the most famous and influential account of Dido's life and tragic love for Aeneas.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Carthage
keyAchievements.description: She led her people from Tyre and, through cleverness and leadership, established one of the ancient world's most powerful and enduring cities.

keyAchievements.title: The Oxhide Bargain
keyAchievements.description: Her legendary trick of cutting an oxhide into thin strips to encircle a large area of land became a symbol of ingenuity and the foundation of her new kingdom.

keyAchievements.title: Archetype of the Tragic Heroine
keyAchievements.description: Her story in Virgil's 'Aeneid' immortalized her as an archetype of a powerful woman consumed by tragic love and betrayal, influencing Western art and literature for centuries.

faq.question: Was Dido a real person?
faq.answer: Her historicity is debated. While some ancient Greek and Roman historians wrote of her as a real figure, the most famous account of her life in Virgil's 'Aeneid' is a literary creation that blends myth with historical elements.

faq.question: Why did Dido kill herself?
faq.answer: In Virgil's 'Aeneid', she commits suicide out of heartbreak after Aeneas abandons her to fulfill his destiny in Italy. In earlier legends, she killed herself to avoid being forced to marry a local king, thus remaining faithful to her deceased husband.

faq.question: What is the significance of Dido's curse?
faq.answer: In the 'Aeneid', Dido's curse on Aeneas and his descendants serves as a mythological origin story for the intense rivalry and the three Punic Wars fought between Carthage and Rome.

timeline.event: Born as Ramavo to non-noble parents.
Ascends to the throne of the Kingdom of Madagascar following the death of her husband, King Radama I, and takes the name Ranavalona.
Issues an edict prohibiting the practice of Christianity in Madagascar, leading to the persecution of Malagasy Christians.
Successfully repels a joint French and British naval attack on the port of Tamatave, demonstrating the strength of her military.
A coup attempt, plotted by her son Radama II and European associates like Jean Laborde and Joseph-François Lambert, is discovered and foiled.
Dies in her sleep, ending her 33-year reign. She is succeeded by her son, Radama II, who reverses her isolationist policies.

keyAchievements.title: Preservation of Sovereignty
keyAchievements.description: Successfully resisted European colonial encroachment for over 30 years, preserving Madagascar's independence long after many other nations had fallen.

keyAchievements.title: Industrial Self-Sufficiency
keyAchievements.description: Established a local industrial complex that produced modern weaponry, textiles, and other goods, reducing reliance on foreign imports.

keyAchievements.title: Military Modernization
keyAchievements.description: Built and maintained a large, well-trained standing army equipped with locally manufactured arms, capable of defending the island from foreign invasion.

faq.question: Why was Ranavalona I called the 'Mad Queen' or 'Female Caligula' by Europeans?
faq.answer: Her policies, particularly the violent persecution of Christians and the extensive use of the 'tangena' poison ordeal which killed thousands, were seen as barbaric and tyrannical from a European perspective. This narrative also served to delegitimize her rule and justify potential colonial intervention.

faq.question: What was the 'tangena' ordeal?
faq.answer: It was a traditional form of trial by ordeal where an accused person was forced to ingest a poison from the nut of the Tangena shrub. Survival was taken as proof of innocence, while death signified guilt. Ranavalona used it extensively to eliminate political opponents and enforce social and religious conformity.

faq.question: Did she truly hate all foreigners?
faq.answer: Not entirely. While she was deeply suspicious of European governments and expelled missionaries, she selectively employed certain foreigners whose skills she deemed essential. The most notable example is Jean Laborde, a Frenchman who was instrumental in developing her industrial and military complex.

faq.question: What was her legacy in Madagascar?
faq.answer: Her legacy is complex and debated. Some view her as a cruel tyrant responsible for the deaths of a large percentage of her population. Others see her as a national hero who fiercely defended Malagasy sovereignty and traditions against the tide of European colonialism, preserving the nation's independence for several decades.

timeline.event: Born in Roha, Ethiopia. Legend states a swarm of bees surrounded him at birth, leading his mother to name him Lalibela ('the bees recognize his sovereignty').
Goes into exile due to conflict with his half-brother, King Harba.
Survives a poisoning attempt, during which he reportedly has a divine vision of the rock-hewn churches.
Reigns as Emperor of Ethiopia after his brother abdicates the throne to him.
Commissions and oversees the construction of the eleven rock-hewn monolithic churches in his capital, which is renamed Lalibela in his honor.
Dies, having abdicated the throne to live out his final days as a hermit. He is later canonized as a saint by the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of the Rock-Hewn Churches
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned the carving of eleven monolithic churches from solid rock, creating a UNESCO World Heritage site.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a 'New Jerusalem'
keyAchievements.description: Established his capital as a major Christian pilgrimage site, intended to be a substitute for Jerusalem.

keyAchievements.title: Canonization as a Saint
keyAchievements.description: Revered as one of the most important saints in the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church for his piety and monumental works of faith.

faq.question: Are the churches of Lalibela really built from a single rock?
faq.answer: Yes, they are monolithic, meaning they were carved from a single, solid block of volcanic tuff rock. They were excavated from the ground down, rather than being built from the ground up.

faq.question: Why is Lalibela called a 'New Jerusalem'?
faq.answer: Emperor Lalibela intended the city to be a substitute pilgrimage site for Christians after the capture of Jerusalem by Saladin in 1187 made travel to the Holy Land difficult and dangerous. The site's layout and church names even mimic those of Jerusalem.

faq.question: Is it true that angels helped build the churches?
faq.answer: According to Ethiopian tradition and hagiography, angels worked alongside the human laborers at night, doubling the progress made each day. This legend explains the seemingly impossible speed and scale of the construction.

timeline.event: Born al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Wazzan in Granada, just before or after the city's fall.
His family is exiled from Granada and settles in Fez, Morocco.
Accompanies his uncle on a diplomatic mission across North Africa, reaching Timbuktu and the Songhai Empire.
Embarks on another major journey, visiting Constantinople and sailing up the Nile in Egypt.
Captured at sea by Christian corsairs and taken to Rome.
Baptized by Pope Leo X in St. Peter's Basilica, taking the name Joannes Leo de Medicis.
Completes the manuscript for his seminal work, 'Description of Africa', in Italian.
Leaves Rome. His later life is uncertain, with some historians believing he returned to Tunis.
His 'Description of Africa' is first published in Venice by Giovanni Battista Ramusio.
The presumed year of his death, though the exact date and location are unknown.

keyAchievements.title: Description of Africa (Della descrittione dell'Africa)
keyAchievements.description: Authored the most comprehensive and influential account of North African geography, culture, and politics available to Europeans for centuries.

keyAchievements.title: Cultural Intermediary
keyAchievements.description: Served as a unique bridge between the Islamic world of North Africa and the Christian world of Renaissance Europe through his life and work.

keyAchievements.title: Polyglot Scholar
keyAchievements.description: Mastered multiple languages including Arabic, Italian, and likely Spanish and Hebrew, which enabled his scholarly and diplomatic work across cultures.

faq.question: Was Leo Africanus his real name?
faq.answer: No, his birth name was al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Wazzan. He was given the name Joannes Leo de Medicis upon his baptism by Pope Leo X, and became known as Leo Africanus (Leo the African) after his famous book.

faq.question: Why was his book so important?
faq.answer: Before his work, European knowledge of the African interior was based on ancient texts and sparse rumors. His book, based on direct travel and observation, provided detailed, first-hand accounts of major cities like Timbuktu, trade routes, political structures, and local customs.

faq.question: Did he remain a Christian?
faq.answer: It is unknown. After leaving Rome around 1529, his trail goes cold. Some historians believe he returned to Tunis in North Africa and may have reconverted to Islam, but there is no definitive proof.

timeline.event: Born in Mosega, Transvaal, son of Mzilikazi, founder of the Ndebele kingdom.
Crowned king after a civil war following his father's death, consolidating his power over Matabeleland.
Grants the Rudd Concession to agents of Cecil Rhodes, unknowingly signing away mineral rights to his entire kingdom.
The First Matabele War begins. The Ndebele are defeated by the British South Africa Company's Maxim guns.
Dies while fleeing north after burning his capital, Bulawayo. His death marks the end of the Ndebele Kingdom's independence.

keyAchievements.title: Consolidation of the Ndebele Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: After a succession crisis, he successfully unified the Ndebele state and ruled for over two decades.

keyAchievements.title: Diplomatic Resistance
keyAchievements.description: He skillfully used diplomacy for years to delay and manage European encroachment, attempting to preserve his kingdom's sovereignty.

faq.question: Was Lobengula tricked into signing the Rudd Concession?
faq.answer: Yes. The verbal agreement discussed limited mining activity, but the written document, which he could not read, granted the British South Africa Company exclusive mineral rights and the power to do whatever was necessary to exploit them, effectively ceding sovereignty.

faq.question: Why did the First Matabele War start?
faq.answer: The British South Africa Company used a raid by Ndebele warriors on a Shona group (who were under Company protection) as a pretext to invade. The company was eager to conquer Matabeleland to secure its mineral wealth.

faq.question: What happened to Lobengula?
faq.answer: After his forces were defeated, he burned his capital, Bulawayo, and fled north. He died during his flight in early 1894, though the exact cause (smallpox, poison, or suicide) remains uncertain. His body was never found by the British.

timeline.event: Born as Sahle Mariam in Ankober, Shewa.
Held as a political prisoner by Emperor Tewodros II at the fortress of Magdala.
Escapes from Magdala and becomes King of Shewa.
Becomes Emperor of Ethiopia upon the death of Yohannes IV, taking the name Menelik II.
Signs the Treaty of Wuchale with Italy, which contains disputed clauses leading to conflict.
Abrogates the Treaty of Wuchale after realizing the Italian deception.
Leads Ethiopian forces to a decisive victory over Italy at the Battle of Adwa, securing Ethiopia's sovereignty.
Oversees a period of significant modernization, including the founding of Addis Ababa, and the introduction of railways, electricity, and a modern postal system.
Dies in Addis Ababa, leaving a legacy as a unifier and modernizer.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Adwa
keyAchievements.description: He led Ethiopia to a decisive victory against Italy in 1896, ensuring Ethiopia remained the only African nation to successfully resist European colonization during the Scramble for Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Modernization of Ethiopia
keyAchievements.description: He founded the modern capital, Addis Ababa, and introduced key technologies like the telephone, telegraph, electricity, and the first national railway.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of the Ethiopian Empire
keyAchievements.description: Through diplomacy and military campaigns, he consolidated disparate regions and peoples into the modern borders of Ethiopia.

faq.question: Why is the Battle of Adwa so significant?
faq.answer: It was the first time a non-European nation had decisively defeated a modern European army in battle. The victory became a powerful symbol of African resistance and independence movements worldwide.

faq.question: What was the controversy over the Treaty of Wuchale?
faq.answer: The Amharic version stated the Emperor could use Italy for foreign relations, while the Italian version made it mandatory, effectively making Ethiopia a protectorate. Menelik II renounced the treaty upon discovering this deception.

faq.question: How did Menelik II come to power?
faq.answer: After escaping a 10-year imprisonment, he became King of Shewa. He then spent over two decades consolidating power and expanding his influence until he was strong enough to claim the imperial throne in 1889.

faq.question: What does the name 'Menelik' mean?
faq.answer: He took the throne name Menelik II in reference to Menelik I, the legendary first emperor of Ethiopia who was said to be the son of the biblical King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba. This name linked his reign to this ancient, prestigious lineage.

timeline.event: Born as Lepoqo near the Maloti Mountains.
Gathers followers during the Difaqane wars and establishes himself as a chief.
Moves his people to the Thaba Bosiu mountain stronghold for better defense.
Welcomes French Protestant missionaries from the Paris Evangelical Missionary Society, who become key advisors.
The First Free State–Basotho War (Senekal's War) begins over land disputes with the Boer Orange Free State.
The Second Free State–Basotho War (Seqiti War) results in significant loss of land for the Basotho.
Successfully petitions Queen Victoria, and Basutoland (modern Lesotho) becomes a British protectorate, saving it from annexation.
Dies at Thaba Bosiu, leaving behind a unified nation.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the Basotho Nation
keyAchievements.description: He united various Sotho-speaking clans and refugees from the Difaqane wars into a cohesive nation.

keyAchievements.title: Masterful Diplomacy
keyAchievements.description: He skillfully used negotiation, alliances, and generosity to manage conflicts with African rivals, Boer settlers, and the British.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Thaba Bosiu
keyAchievements.description: He chose and fortified the Thaba Bosiu plateau, a natural fortress that was never conquered in his lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: Securing British Protection
keyAchievements.description: His greatest diplomatic achievement was making Basutoland a British protectorate, ensuring its survival as a distinct entity.

faq.question: Why is Moshoeshoe I considered the founder of Lesotho?
faq.answer: He brought together disparate groups of people fleeing the chaos of the Difaqane, gave them a shared identity, and established the political and geographical foundations of the modern nation of Lesotho.

faq.question: How did he deal with his enemies?
faq.answer: Moshoeshoe used a dual strategy. He defended his mountain fortress of Thaba Bosiu militarily, but was famous for his diplomacy, often turning defeated enemies into loyal subjects by offering them land, food, and protection.

faq.question: What is the significance of Thaba Bosiu?
faq.answer: Thaba Bosiu ('Mountain at Night') was his capital and fortress. Its natural defenses made it nearly impregnable, allowing his small nation to withstand sieges from much larger and better-armed forces.

faq.question: Why did he seek an alliance with European missionaries?
faq.answer: He was a pragmatist who saw missionaries as a source of valuable knowledge, literacy, and technology (like guns and plows). They also served as his diplomatic secretaries and intermediaries with other European powers.

timeline.event: Born on Labab Island, near Dongola in Sudan.
Gains a reputation as a respected Sufi religious scholar and ascetic.
Proclaims himself the Mahdi ('the Guided One') and begins calling for a jihad against the Turco-Egyptian administration.
His forces, the Ansar, capture the major city of El Obeid, a turning point in the war.
Lays siege to Khartoum, the capital of Turco-Egyptian Sudan, defended by British General Charles Gordon.
The Ansar capture Khartoum, killing General Gordon and effectively ending foreign rule.
Dies of typhus in his new capital, Omdurman, just six months after his greatest victory.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Sudan
keyAchievements.description: He successfully united disparate Sudanese tribes and clans under the banner of his religious movement.

keyAchievements.title: Mahdist War Victory
keyAchievements.description: Led a successful revolutionary war against the technologically superior Turco-Egyptian and British forces.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Mahdist State
keyAchievements.description: Founded a vast, independent Islamic state (the Mahdiyya) in Sudan that lasted for 15 years after his death.

faq.question: What does the title 'Mahdi' mean?
faq.answer: In Islam, the Mahdi is a prophesied redeemer or 'guided one' who will appear before the Day of Judgment to rid the world of evil and injustice.

faq.question: Why did he rebel against the Turco-Egyptian government?
faq.answer: He was motivated by widespread resentment of the government's high taxes, corruption, and its perceived lax adherence to Islamic law.

faq.question: What was the significance of the fall of Khartoum?
faq.answer: It marked the decisive victory of the Mahdist forces, ended Turco-Egyptian rule in Sudan, and the death of British General Gordon caused a major political crisis in Britain.

faq.question: What happened to the Mahdist State after his death?
faq.answer: His successor, Khalifa Abdallahi ibn Muhammad, ruled the state until it was conquered by an Anglo-Egyptian force in 1899, leading to the establishment of Anglo-Egyptian Sudan.

timeline.event: Born as Prince Kondo in Abomey, the capital of the Kingdom of Dahomey.
Ascends to the throne upon the death of his father, King Glele, and takes the name Behanzin.
The First Franco-Dahomean War begins. Behanzin's forces, including the Dahomey Amazons, fight the French to a stalemate.
The Second Franco-Dahomean War begins. French forces launch a larger invasion.
French forces capture the capital, Abomey. Behanzin escapes and begins a guerrilla war.
To prevent further bloodshed, Behanzin surrenders to the French.
Exiled by the French, first to Martinique and later to Algeria.
Dies in exile in Algiers, Algeria. His remains were repatriated to Dahomey (now Benin) in 1928.

keyAchievements.title: Military Resistance
keyAchievements.description: Led a fierce and organized resistance against the technologically superior French colonial army for over four years.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Anti-Colonialism
keyAchievements.description: Became a powerful and enduring symbol of African resistance to European colonization, celebrated across the continent.

keyAchievements.title: Command of the Dahomey Amazons
keyAchievements.description: Commanded the 'Mino' or Dahomey Amazons, one of history's only documented frontline female combat units, who were renowned for their bravery.

faq.question: Why did Dahomey go to war with France?
faq.answer: The wars were caused by French colonial expansion and disputes over territory and sovereignty, particularly France's claim to the port city of Cotonou, which was a key source of revenue for Dahomey.

faq.question: Who were the Dahomey Amazons?
faq.answer: The Dahomey Amazons, or 'Mino', were an all-female military regiment of the Kingdom of Dahomey. They were known for their exceptional bravery and skill in combat and played a major role in the wars against the French.

faq.question: What happened to Behanzin after he surrendered?
faq.answer: Instead of being allowed to remain in his kingdom under French rule, he was exiled. He was sent to Martinique in the Caribbean and then to Algeria in North Africa, where he lived until his death in 1906.

faq.question: Why is he called the 'Shark King'?
faq.answer: He was given the throne name Behanzin, which is often associated with the proverb 'The angry shark will stir up the bar,' referring to a lagoon barrier. It symbolized his power and his defiant stance against the French, like a shark troubling the waters.

timeline.event: 가르비야 주 이비아나에서 태어남.
항소 법원 판사로 임명됨.
교육부 장관으로 임명됨.
법무부 장관으로 임명됨.
영국으로부터의 독립을 요구하기 위해 와프드(대표단)를 결성함.
체포되어 몰타로 추방당했으며, 이는 1919년 이집트 혁명을 촉발시킴.
영국이 이집트의 명목상 독립을 일방적으로 선언함. 자글룰은 두 번째 추방 상태에 있었음.
이집트 최초의 민선 총리가 됨.
카이로에서 사망함.

keyAchievements.title: 와프드당 창당
keyAchievements.description: 이집트에서 가장 영향력 있는 민족주의 정당을 창설하여 독립을 위해 대중을 동원함.

keyAchievements.title: 1919년 혁명 주도
keyAchievements.description: 추방 중에도 그의 리더십은 이집트 국민의 통일된 자유 요구를 보여주는 전국적인 봉기를 고무시킴.

keyAchievements.title: 명목상 독립 쟁취
keyAchievements.description: 그의 끈질긴 정치적 압박과 협상은 영국이 보호령을 종식시키는 1922년 일방적 선언을 하도록 강제함.

keyAchievements.title: 최초의 민선 총리
keyAchievements.description: 새 헌법에 따라 이집트 최초의 총리가 되어 의회 민주주의를 향한 중요한 발걸음을 내디딤.

faq.question: 왜 그가 '국부'로 불렸나요?
faq.answer: 그는 이집트 민족주의 운동의 중심 인물이었으며, 그의 리더십과 개인적인 희생은 영국 보호령의 종식과 현대적인 반독립 이집트 국가의 탄생으로 직접 이어졌기 때문입니다.

faq.question: 1919년 혁명의 의의는 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 20세기 최초의 성공적인 대중 반식민 봉기 중 하나로, 비폭력 시민 불복종과 대중 시위의 힘을 보여주었으며, 이집트와 영국 간의 관계를 근본적으로 바꾸었습니다.

faq.question: 1922년 이후 이집트는 진정으로 독립했나요?
faq.answer: 아니요, 독립은 명목상이었습니다. 영국은 수에즈 운하의 안보, 국방, 외국인 이익 보호, 수단 등 중요한 영역에 대한 통제권을 유지했습니다. 완전한 독립은 수십 년이 더 지나서야 이루어졌습니다.

timeline.event: Born in the Konyan region of what is now Guinea.
His mother is captured in a slave raid, prompting him to begin a military career to secure her release.
Proclaims himself Almami (leader) and officially founds the Wassoulou Empire.
First major military conflict with French colonial forces.
Conducts a strategic 'scorched earth' retreat, moving his entire empire eastward to escape French encirclement.
Captured by French Captain Henri Gouraud in a surprise attack at Guélémou.
Dies of pneumonia while in exile on the island of Ndjolé in Gabon.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the Wassoulou Empire
keyAchievements.description: He unified numerous small West African states into a single, powerful Islamic empire under his rule.

keyAchievements.title: Military Innovator
keyAchievements.description: He created a modern, professional army equipped with European firearms and supported by local weapon-repair workshops.

keyAchievements.title: Leader of Anti-Colonial Resistance
keyAchievements.description: He led one of the longest and most effective resistance campaigns against French colonial expansion in Africa, lasting nearly two decades.

faq.question: Why is Samori Toure considered a national hero in Guinea?
faq.answer: He is celebrated for his military genius, state-building, and fierce, prolonged resistance against European colonization, making him a symbol of African sovereignty and pride.

faq.question: How was he finally defeated?
faq.answer: He was defeated by a combination of factors: the French army's superior long-term resources, a devastating famine that weakened his empire, and a final surprise attack that led to his capture in 1898.

faq.question: Did he have any connection to modern Guinea?
faq.answer: Yes, his great-grandson, Ahmed Sékou Touré, was the first President of Guinea after it gained independence from France in 1958, continuing his legacy of fighting for Guinean sovereignty.

timeline.event: Born Ajayi in Osogun, Yorubaland (in present-day Nigeria).
Captured by Fulani slave raiders and sold into slavery.
Rescued from a Portuguese slave ship by the British Royal Navy and taken to Freetown, Sierra Leone.
Baptized into the Anglican church, taking the name Samuel Crowther.
Participated in the British Niger expedition as an interpreter and missionary.
Ordained as a minister in England by the Church Missionary Society and published 'A Vocabulary of the Yoruba Language'.
Consecrated in Canterbury Cathedral as the first African Anglican bishop.
The complete Yoruba Bible (Bibeli Mimo), a translation project he led, was published.
Died in Lagos on December 31st.

keyAchievements.title: First African Anglican Bishop
keyAchievements.description: Consecrated in 1864, he was a pioneer of indigenous leadership in the African church, overseeing the Niger Mission.

keyAchievements.title: Yoruba Bible Translation
keyAchievements.description: He led the monumental project of translating the entire Bible into the Yoruba language, a cornerstone of Yoruba literature and Christianity.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Yoruba Linguistics
keyAchievements.description: He published the first comprehensive grammar and vocabulary of the Yoruba language, standardizing its written form and enabling widespread literacy.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Indigenous Missions
keyAchievements.description: He established and led the Niger Mission, which was staffed entirely by Africans, promoting a model of self-governing African Christianity.

faq.question: Was Samuel Ajayi Crowther born a Christian?
faq.answer: No, he was born into a Yoruba family and followed traditional Yoruba religion. He converted to Christianity in Sierra Leone after being freed from a slave ship.

faq.question: Why is his linguistic work so important?
faq.answer: His work standardized the Yoruba language, creating a unified script and grammar. This allowed for widespread literacy, the preservation of culture, and the creation of foundational texts like the Yoruba Bible, which is still in use today.

faq.question: Did he face challenges as a bishop?
faq.answer: Yes, particularly in his later years. He faced significant prejudice from younger European missionaries who questioned his authority and methods, ultimately leading to the dismantling of his all-African Niger Mission by the Church Missionary Society.

faq.question: How was he captured and freed?
faq.answer: He was captured at age 13 by Fulani slave raiders from his village. After being sold several times, he was put on a Portuguese ship bound for the Americas, which was intercepted by the British Royal Navy's anti-slavery patrol, and he was freed in Sierra Leone.

timeline.event: Born in the Buuhoodle region, in what is now modern-day Ethiopia, near the border with Somaliland.
Travels to Mecca and Medina, where he joins the Salihiyya Sufi order, deepening his religious and political convictions.
Declares a jihad against the colonial powers, marking the official beginning of the Dervish resistance movement.
Leads the Dervish forces in four major expeditions against the British, Ethiopians, and Italians, establishing his reputation as a formidable military leader.
Establishes his capital at Taleh, a large fortified complex of forts, which becomes the center of the Dervish state.
The British Royal Air Force (RAF) launches a successful aerial bombardment of Taleh and other Dervish forts, crushing the movement's infrastructure.
Dies of influenza while regrouping his scattered forces in the Ogaden region, ending the 20-year Dervish War.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the Dervish State
keyAchievements.description: He united Somali clans to form an independent state that resisted three colonial powers for two decades.

keyAchievements.title: Master Poet and Orator
keyAchievements.description: He used powerful poetry (gabay) to inspire nationalism, recruit followers, and document his struggle against imperialism.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Anti-Colonial Resistance
keyAchievements.description: He became a foundational figure of Somali nationalism, inspiring future generations to fight for independence and sovereignty.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Modern Guerrilla Warfare
keyAchievements.description: His forces successfully employed hit-and-run tactics and strategic retreats against technologically superior European armies.

faq.question: Why was he called the 'Mad Mullah' by the British?
faq.answer: It was a derogatory term used by the British to discredit his religious authority and portray him as an irrational fanatic, thereby undermining his legitimate nationalist and anti-colonial movement.

faq.question: How was his movement finally defeated?
faq.answer: After 20 years of inconclusive ground warfare, the British employed aerial bombardment for the first time in a colonial conflict in Africa, destroying his forts in 1920 and forcing his forces to scatter.

faq.question: What is his legacy in Somalia today?
faq.answer: He is revered as a national hero and the 'Father of Somali Nationalism' for his role in resisting foreign domination. Statues, schools, and even an airport have been named in his honor.

faq.question: Was his movement purely religious?
faq.answer: While deeply rooted in his Salihiyya Islamic faith, his movement was equally a nationalist one. He sought to unite all Somalis, regardless of clan, to create a unified, independent state free from foreign rule.

timeline.event: Ascends to the throne of the Songhai kingdom in Gao.
Conquers the city of Timbuktu from the Tuareg.
Captures the city of Djenné after a seven-year siege.
Conducts numerous military campaigns, expanding the Songhai Empire across the Niger Bend.
Dies by drowning in the Koni River, ending his 28-year reign.

keyAchievements.title: Founding the Songhai Empire
keyAchievements.description: Transformed the small kingdom of Gao into one of West Africa's largest and most powerful empires.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of a River Navy
keyAchievements.description: Built a large fleet of war canoes on the Niger River, enabling rapid troop movement and control of trade.

keyAchievements.title: Control of Trans-Saharan Trade
keyAchievements.description: Captured the key trading hubs of Timbuktu and Djenné, securing immense wealth for the empire.

faq.question: Why was Sonni Ali considered a controversial figure?
faq.answer: While a brilliant military leader, he was criticized by Muslim chroniclers in Timbuktu for his harsh conquest of the city and his adherence to traditional African religious practices alongside Islam.

faq.question: How did Sonni Ali die?
faq.answer: He is believed to have drowned while crossing the Koni River (a tributary of the Niger) in 1492 upon returning from a military expedition.

faq.question: What was his relationship with Islam?
faq.answer: Sonni Ali was nominally a Muslim, but he practiced a syncretic form of religion that blended Islamic tenets with traditional Songhai beliefs, which put him at odds with orthodox Muslim scholars.

timeline.event: Born in Boshof, Orange Free State.
Moves to Kimberley and works as a post office messenger.
Becomes a court interpreter in Mafeking during the Second Boer War.
Founds the first Tswana-English newspaper, Koranta ea Becoana.
Becomes a founding member and first General Secretary of the South African Native National Congress (SANNC).
The Natives' Land Act is passed, dispossessing black South Africans of their land.
Travels to Britain with an SANNC delegation to protest the Land Act.
Publishes "Native Life in South Africa" to expose the effects of the Land Act.
Publishes "Mhudi," one of the first full-length novels in English by a black African.
Dies of pneumonia in Johannesburg.

keyAchievements.title: Co-founding the ANC
keyAchievements.description: He was a founding member and the first General Secretary of the South African Native National Congress (SANNC), the precursor to the African National Congress (ANC).

keyAchievements.title: Native Life in South Africa
keyAchievements.description: Authored a powerful journalistic and political book exposing the devastating impact of the 1913 Natives' Land Act.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of African Literature
keyAchievements.description: Wrote "Mhudi," one of the first novels by a black South African in English, and translated Shakespeare's works into the Tswana language.

faq.question: What was the main focus of Sol Plaatje's activism?
faq.answer: His primary focus was fighting against racial segregation and the dispossession of land from black South Africans, most notably through his opposition to the 1913 Natives' Land Act.

faq.question: Why is he considered a polyglot?
faq.answer: Plaatje was fluent in at least seven languages, including his native Tswana, English, Dutch, German, Sotho, Zulu, and Xhosa, which he used in his work as a journalist, writer, and court interpreter.

faq.question: What is the significance of his novel "Mhudi"?
faq.answer: "Mhudi" is significant as one of the first novels written in English by a black South African. It reclaims African history from a pre-colonial perspective, centering on the Barolong people during the 19th century.

timeline.event: Birth of Susenyos.
Captured by Oromo raiders, later escapes.
Crowned Emperor of Ethiopia after defeating his rivals.
Publicly converts to Roman Catholicism and declares it the state religion.
A decade of civil war and rebellion against his religious policies.
Revokes his edict, restores the Ethiopian Orthodox faith.
Abdicates in favor of his son, Fasilides.
Dies and is buried according to Orthodox tradition.

keyAchievements.title: Military Campaigns
keyAchievements.description: Successfully led numerous military campaigns to consolidate power and defend Ethiopia's borders against the Oromo and other threats.

keyAchievements.title: Proclamation of Liberty of Conscience
keyAchievements.description: In his final act as emperor, he restored religious freedom, ending a decade of civil war.

keyAchievements.title: Architectural Patronage
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned the construction of several significant buildings, including churches and a palace at Gorgora, blending Ethiopian and European styles.

faq.question: Why did Susenyos I convert to Catholicism?
faq.answer: He converted primarily for political and military reasons, seeking an alliance with Portugal and Spain for modern weapons against the Oromo and Ottomans. He was also genuinely persuaded by the theological arguments of Jesuit missionary Pedro Páez.

faq.question: Was his reign a failure?
faq.answer: It's complex. While his religious policy led to a devastating civil war, his reign also saw military successes and cultural exchange. His ultimate decision to abdicate to save his people from further bloodshed is often seen as a final act of wisdom and sacrifice.

faq.question: What happened after he abdicated?
faq.answer: His son, Fasilides, became emperor. Fasilides promptly expelled the Jesuits, severed ties with Europe, and re-established the Ethiopian Orthodox Church's dominance. He moved the capital to Gondar, ushering in a new era of isolation and cultural flourishing.

timeline.event: Ascended to the throne of the Kingdom of Kush, holding the title of Kandake (Queen).
Augustus becomes the first Roman Emperor and annexes neighboring Egypt, bringing Rome to Kush's border.
The Kushite-Roman war begins. Amanirenas leads an attack on Roman-held cities in southern Egypt.
Roman prefect Gaius Petronius retaliates, pushing into Kush and sacking the city of Napata. Amanirenas is wounded in battle, losing an eye.
After years of conflict, a peace treaty is negotiated. The Kushite delegation meets with Emperor Augustus on the island of Samos.
The favorable peace treaty is finalized, cancelling Roman tribute demands and establishing a permanent border, securing Kushite independence.
Approximate year of her death. She is buried in her pyramid at Jebel Barkal.

keyAchievements.title: War Against Rome
keyAchievements.description: Successfully led the Kushite army against the formidable Roman Empire for several years, achieving strategic victories.

keyAchievements.title: Favorable Peace Treaty
keyAchievements.description: Negotiated a peace treaty with Emperor Augustus that cancelled Roman demands for tribute and established a permanent, respected border.

keyAchievements.title: Preservation of Sovereignty
keyAchievements.description: Her actions ensured the Kingdom of Kush remained an independent and prosperous power for centuries after the conflict.

keyAchievements.title: The Meroë Head
keyAchievements.description: Captured a bronze head from a statue of Augustus and buried it under a temple, a powerful symbol of her victory over Rome.

faq.question: Was Amanirenas really blind in one eye?
faq.answer: Yes, the ancient Roman historian Strabo described her as 'a masculine sort of woman, and blind in one eye,' confirming she lost an eye as a result of a battle wound.

faq.question: What does 'Kandake' mean?
faq.answer: Kandake, or Candace, was the title for a queen, queen regent, or queen mother of the Kingdom of Kush. These women often held immense political and military power.

faq.question: What happened to the statue head of Augustus she captured?
faq.answer: The 'Meroë Head,' a larger-than-life bronze head from a statue of Augustus, was discovered by archaeologists in 1910. It was found buried under the steps of a temple in Meroë, exactly as ancient accounts suggested.

faq.question: Did Kush have to pay tribute to Rome after the war?
faq.answer: No. A key outcome of the peace treaty negotiated by Amanirenas was the complete cancellation of any tribute payments to Rome, a major diplomatic victory.

timeline.event: Born in Mbacké, Senegal, to a family of Qadiriyya scholars.
Founded the Mouride brotherhood and the city of Touba.
Arrested by French colonial authorities and exiled to Gabon.
Returned to Senegal from his first exile.
Exiled for a second time, to Mauritania, for four years.
Returned from his second exile and was placed under house arrest in Thiéyène and later Diourbel.
Passed away in Diourbel. His body was interred in the Great Mosque of Touba.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the Mouride Brotherhood
keyAchievements.description: He established one of the most prominent and influential Sufi orders in Senegal and West Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Non-Violent Resistance
keyAchievements.description: He resisted French colonialism through spiritual teachings and the promotion of a disciplined work ethic, not armed conflict.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Touba
keyAchievements.description: He founded the city of Touba, which became the holy city of Mouridism and the site of one of Africa's largest religious pilgrimages.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet and Scholar
keyAchievements.description: He authored a vast collection of poems and religious texts, known as 'qasā'id', which are central to Mouride devotion.

faq.question: Why were the French colonial authorities suspicious of Ahmadu Bamba?
faq.answer: They feared his immense spiritual influence over the Senegalese people could incite a large-scale jihad or political uprising against their rule, misinterpreting his spiritual authority as a threat to their control.

faq.question: What is the Grand Magal of Touba?
faq.answer: It is an annual pilgrimage to the city of Touba, commemorating the date of Ahmadu Bamba's exile in 1895. It is the largest religious gathering in Senegal, drawing millions of his followers.

faq.question: What does the name 'Mouride' mean?
faq.answer: The name 'Mouride' is derived from the Arabic word 'murīd', which means 'one who desires' or 'aspirant'. It refers to a disciple who seeks spiritual knowledge and closeness to God under the guidance of a spiritual master.

timeline.event: Ascended to the throne as Amenhotep IV in Thebes.
Began his religious revolution, promoting the Aten as the supreme, and later sole, god.
Changed his name to Akhenaten and began construction of his new capital city, Akhetaten (modern Amarna).
The royal court officially moved from Thebes to the new capital of Akhetaten.
Died after a 17-year reign. His religious reforms were soon dismantled under his successors.
His tomb, KV55 in the Valley of the Kings, was discovered, containing a mummy believed to be his.

keyAchievements.title: Atenism
keyAchievements.description: He established one of history's first recorded monotheistic (or monolatristic) religions, centered on the worship of the sun disk, Aten.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Akhetaten (Amarna)
keyAchievements.description: He conceived and built an entirely new capital city in the desert, dedicated to the Aten, which was abandoned shortly after his death.

keyAchievements.title: Amarna Art Style
keyAchievements.description: His reign produced a unique and revolutionary art style characterized by naturalism, intimacy, and a departure from traditional rigid portrayals.

faq.question: Was Akhenaten the world's first monotheist?
faq.answer: He is one of the earliest and most famous examples of a ruler imposing a monotheistic (or more accurately, monolatristic) state religion. While the concept may have existed before, his large-scale, state-sponsored reform is unique for its time.

faq.question: What happened to his wife, Nefertiti?
faq.answer: Nefertiti was a powerful figure who featured prominently alongside Akhenaten. Her fate is a mystery; she disappears from historical records around year 12 of his reign. Some theories suggest she may have ruled briefly as a pharaoh under a different name after his death.

faq.question: Why was his legacy erased?
faq.answer: After his death, the powerful priesthood of Amun-Ra was restored. His successors, most notably Horemheb, undertook a campaign to dismantle his temples, deface his monuments, and remove his name from king lists to erase the 'heretical' Amarna period from history.

faq.question: Did Akhenaten's religion influence Judaism?
faq.answer: This is a topic of scholarly debate. Some elements of Akhenaten's 'Great Hymn to the Aten' bear a striking resemblance to Psalm 104 in the Bible, leading some scholars, like Sigmund Freud, to theorize a connection, though direct influence is not proven.

timeline.event: Born in Araouane, near Timbuktu, into the scholarly Aqit family.
Became a prominent teacher and jurist at the Sankoré Madrasa in Timbuktu, establishing a vast personal library.
The Moroccan Saadi dynasty invades the Songhai Empire, winning the decisive Battle of Tondibi and sacking Timbuktu.
Arrested by Moroccan forces on charges of sedition for refusing to cooperate with the new rulers.
Deported across the Sahara and exiled to Marrakesh, Morocco. His library of over 1600 books is confiscated.
Released from prison in Marrakesh but kept under house arrest, where he continued to teach and attract Moroccan scholars.
After years of petitioning, he is finally allowed to return to Timbuktu.
Dies in Timbuktu, having spent his final years teaching and writing amidst the ruins of his city's golden age.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Author
keyAchievements.description: He authored over 40 works on Islamic law (fiqh), Arabic grammar, and theology, becoming one of the most respected scholars of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Biographical Dictionary
keyAchievements.description: He wrote 'Nayl al-Ibtihaj bi Tatriz al-Dibaj', a major biographical dictionary of scholars from the Maliki school of law, which remains a vital historical source.

keyAchievements.title: Jurisprudence on Slavery
keyAchievements.description: His treatise 'Mi'raj al-su'ud' (The Ladder of Ascent) provided a key legal argument against the enslavement of free Muslims based on race or ethnicity.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Resistance
keyAchievements.description: His intellectual defiance against the Moroccan invasion made him a powerful symbol of Timbuktu's lost sovereignty and scholarly heritage.

faq.question: Why was Ahmad Baba arrested?
faq.answer: He was a prominent intellectual and leader in Timbuktu who refused to pledge allegiance to the Moroccan invaders. He was accused of inciting rebellion and became a symbol of resistance.

faq.question: What happened to his famous library?
faq.answer: His personal library, said to contain over 1600 volumes, was confiscated by the Moroccan forces upon his arrest. This was considered a catastrophic loss of accumulated knowledge for the entire region.

faq.question: Was he well-known outside of Timbuktu?
faq.answer: Yes. Even during his exile in Marrakesh, his immense knowledge was recognized. Scholars and students flocked to him, and he engaged in debates with the leading jurists of Morocco, earning their respect.

faq.question: What is his most famous work?
faq.answer: While his biographical dictionary 'Nayl al-Ibtihaj' is historically crucial, his legal opinion on slavery, 'Mi'raj al-su'ud', is arguably his most influential work, cited for centuries in debates on slavery and race in the Islamic world.

timeline.event: Born to King Ella Amida of Aksum.
Ascends to the throne as a child after his father's death, with his mother, Sofya, as regent. Frumentius, a Syrian Christian, becomes his tutor.
Reaches adulthood and begins his personal rule. Conducts military campaigns against neighboring peoples, including the Beja and in Nubia.
Converts to Christianity. Aksum becomes one of the first states in the world to officially adopt Christianity as its state religion.
Frumentius is consecrated as the first Bishop of Aksum by Athanasius of Alexandria, solidifying the link between the Ethiopian and Coptic churches.
Death of Ezana. His reign marks the zenith of Aksumite power and influence.

keyAchievements.title: Christianization of Aksum
keyAchievements.description: Officially adopted Christianity as the state religion, fundamentally shaping the cultural and religious identity of Ethiopia.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Led successful military campaigns that expanded Aksum's territory and influence over Nubia, Yemen, and surrounding regions.

keyAchievements.title: Economic Prosperity
keyAchievements.description: His rule coincided with Aksum's peak as a major trading power, controlling Red Sea routes and minting its own currency (including the first coins to feature the Cross).

faq.question: Why is Ezana so important?
faq.answer: He made Christianity the state religion of Aksum, a decision that created the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church and has defined Ethiopian identity for over 1,600 years.

faq.question: Did he convert for purely religious reasons?
faq.answer: While his personal faith seems genuine, his conversion was also a strategic political move. It aligned Aksum with the powerful Christian Roman Empire and provided a unifying ideology for his diverse kingdom.

faq.question: What is the Ezana Stone?
faq.answer: It is a stone monument, similar to the Rosetta Stone, with the same inscription carved in three languages: Ge'ez, Sabaean, and Greek. It documents his conversion and military victories.

timeline.event: Born in Axim on the Gold Coast (modern-day Ghana).
Brought to Amsterdam and then presented to the court of Duke Anton Ulrich of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel in Germany.
Enrolled at the University of Halle to study law and philosophy.
Publicly defended his dissertation 'De Jure Maurorum in Europa' (On the Rights of Moors in Europe).
Earned his doctorate in philosophy from the University of Wittenberg with the thesis 'On the Apathy of the Human Mind'.
Worked as a lecturer (Privatdozent) in philosophy at the University of Halle.
Lectured in philosophy at the University of Jena.
Returned to the Gold Coast (Ghana) after facing increased prejudice and the loss of his patron.
Died in Ghana, reportedly near the town of Shama.

keyAchievements.title: First Sub-Saharan African University Student in Europe
keyAchievements.description: He was the first known sub-Saharan African to enroll and study at a European university.

keyAchievements.title: Doctorate in Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: He earned a doctorate in philosophy from the University of Wittenberg in 1734, a pioneering achievement.

keyAchievements.title: University Lecturer
keyAchievements.description: He taught philosophy as a lecturer at the prestigious universities of Halle and Jena in Germany.

keyAchievements.title: Early Advocate for African Rights
keyAchievements.description: His 1729 dissertation argued for the legal rights and personhood of Africans in Europe.

faq.question: What was Anton Wilhelm Amo's main philosophical argument?
faq.answer: Amo argued for a strict mind-body dualism. He posited that the mind is an immaterial, active substance that is the true subject of sensation and consciousness, while the body is a material, passive instrument that merely transmits physical stimuli.

faq.question: Why did he have a German name?
faq.answer: He was given the names Anton Wilhelm upon his baptism in Germany, which was sponsored by his patrons, Duke Anton Ulrich and his son, Duke August Wilhelm of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel.

faq.question: Why did he return to Africa?
faq.answer: The exact reasons are not fully documented, but historians believe it was a combination of factors, including the death of his noble patron, increasing racial prejudice and professional rivalries in Germany, and a desire to return to his homeland.

faq.question: Are any of his works still available?
faq.answer: Yes, Latin versions and modern translations of his two main dissertations, 'On the Rights of Moors in Europe' and 'On the Apathy of the Human Mind', have survived and are studied by scholars today.

timeline.event: Born in Alexandria, Roman Egypt.
Attends the First Council of Nicaea as a deacon, playing a key role in opposing Arianism.
Succeeds Alexander as the bishop (or Pope) of Alexandria.
First exile to Trier in Gaul by Emperor Constantine I.
Second exile to Rome under Emperor Constantius II.
Third exile, flees to the Egyptian desert to live among the Desert Fathers.
Fourth exile under Emperor Julian the Apostate.
Fifth and final exile under Emperor Valens.
Dies peacefully in Alexandria after a life of conflict and theological defense.

keyAchievements.title: Champion of Nicene Orthodoxy
keyAchievements.description: He was the foremost defender of the divinity of Christ against the widespread Arian heresy, ensuring the Nicene Creed's survival.

keyAchievements.title: Author of 'On the Incarnation'
keyAchievements.description: This work is a foundational text of Christian theology, explaining why God became human in Jesus Christ to redeem humanity.

keyAchievements.title: Biographer of Antony the Great
keyAchievements.description: His 'Life of Antony' became a bestseller of its time, popularizing the ideals of monasticism throughout the Roman Empire.

faq.question: What is Arianism?
faq.answer: Arianism was a major 4th-century theological doctrine which taught that Jesus Christ was a created being, subordinate to God the Father, and not co-eternal or of the same substance (homoousios).

faq.question: Why was he called 'Athanasius contra mundum'?
faq.answer: This Latin phrase means 'Athanasius against the world.' It reflects his lonely and steadfast opposition to Arianism at a time when it was supported by Roman emperors and a majority of bishops.

faq.question: How many times was Athanasius exiled?
faq.answer: He was exiled five times by four different Roman emperors over a period of 45 years as bishop, spending a total of over 17 years away from his see in Alexandria.

timeline.event: Born in Didouseya, Egypt.
Succeeds his uncle, Theophilus, as Patriarch of Alexandria.
Height of conflict with the Roman prefect Orestes; the philosopher Hypatia is murdered by a Christian mob.
Begins his correspondence and conflict with Nestorius, Patriarch of Constantinople, over the term 'Theotokos'.
Presides over the First Council of Ephesus, which condemns Nestorianism and affirms Mary as Theotokos.
Reaches an agreement with John of Antioch, the 'Formula of Union,' healing a temporary schism.
Dies in Alexandria.

keyAchievements.title: Championing the 'Theotokos' Doctrine
keyAchievements.description: Successfully argued for and codified the title 'Theotokos' (God-bearer) for the Virgin Mary at the Council of Ephesus, defining Christ's divine nature from conception.

keyAchievements.title: Defeating Nestorianism
keyAchievements.description: His theological writings and actions at the Council of Ephesus led to the condemnation of Nestorianism as a heresy.

keyAchievements.title: Doctor of the Church
keyAchievements.description: His extensive writings on Christology and the Trinity earned him the title 'Doctor of the Church' for his profound impact on Christian doctrine.

faq.question: Was Cyril responsible for the murder of Hypatia?
faq.answer: Direct responsibility is unproven and debated by historians. However, his fiery rhetoric against pagans and his political conflict with the prefect Orestes (whom Hypatia supported) created the volatile atmosphere in which a Christian mob murdered her. He is, at the very least, indirectly associated with the tragedy.

faq.question: What was the main point of disagreement with Nestorius?
faq.answer: The core issue was the nature of Jesus Christ. Nestorius argued for a looser union of two distinct persons (divine and human) in Christ and preferred the title 'Christotokos' (Christ-bearer) for Mary. Cyril insisted on a 'hypostatic union' of two natures in one divine person, making 'Theotokos' (God-bearer) the only appropriate title.

faq.question: Why is he called a 'Doctor of the Church'?
faq.answer: This title is given to saints whose theological writings are considered of universal importance to the Church. Cyril's works, especially his defense of the hypostatic union and the Incarnation, were foundational for subsequent Christological doctrine in both Eastern and Western Christianity.

timeline.event: Ascends to the throne of the Ethiopian Empire, succeeding his father Wedem Arad.
Defeats and kills Haq ad-Din I of the Sultanate of Ifat, beginning a major series of campaigns against neighboring Muslim states.
Conducts a major campaign against the Sultanate of Ifat and other Muslim provinces, securing a decisive victory and expanding the empire significantly.
Faces excommunication by the head of the Ethiopian Church over his marriage, but maintains his political and military power.
Dies, leaving behind a vastly expanded and powerful empire that dominated the Horn of Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Military Expansion of Ethiopia
keyAchievements.description: Transformed Ethiopia from a highland kingdom into a dominant empire in the Horn of Africa through extensive military conquests.

keyAchievements.title: Subjugation of the Sultanates
keyAchievements.description: Successfully defeated and subjugated several rival Muslim sultanates, most notably the Sultanate of Ifat, securing Christian dominance in the region.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage of Literature
keyAchievements.description: His reign saw a flourishing of Ge'ez literature, including the composition of the royal chronicles and likely the final form of the 'Kebra Nagast'.

faq.question: Was Amda Seyon a religious crusader?
faq.answer: While he was a devout Christian and his wars had a strong religious dimension against Muslim states, they were also driven by political and economic motives to control trade routes and consolidate power.

faq.question: How did he manage such a large, diverse empire?
faq.answer: He used a system of 'garrison towns' to maintain military control and appointed loyal governors, often from his own court, to rule conquered provinces, centralizing power.

faq.question: What is the main historical source for his reign?
faq.answer: The primary source is a contemporary chronicle, 'The Glorious Victories of Amda Seyon', which details his military campaigns in a hagiographic style, celebrating his triumphs.

timeline.event: Born in Cairo, Egypt.
Begins his chronicle, recording significant events.
Witnesses the French invasion of Egypt under Napoleon Bonaparte and is appointed to the Diwan.
The French are expelled from Egypt by Ottoman and British forces.
Muhammad Ali Pasha establishes his rule over Egypt.
His son is murdered, an event believed to be retaliation by Muhammad Ali for al-Jabarti's critical writings.
Dies in Cairo under mysterious circumstances.

keyAchievements.title: Aja'ib al-athar fi'l-tarajim wa'l-akhbar
keyAchievements.description: Authored a monumental chronicle ('Marvelous Compositions of Biographies and Events') that serves as the definitive primary source for Egyptian history from 1688 to 1821.

keyAchievements.title: Eyewitness to Invasion
keyAchievements.description: Provided a unique and invaluable non-European perspective on the Napoleonic invasion and the clash between European and Islamic civilizations.

faq.question: What was al-Jabarti's relationship with the French?
faq.answer: It was complex. He was appointed to a council by them and was impressed by their science, but he was also deeply critical of their customs, their lack of piety, and the brutality of their occupation.

faq.question: Why is his work so important?
faq.answer: His chronicle is one of the very few detailed, local, and contemporary accounts of a pivotal period in Egyptian history, offering insights into society, politics, and daily life that would otherwise be lost.

faq.question: Did he oppose Muhammad Ali?
faq.answer: Yes, his later writings were highly critical of Muhammad Ali's authoritarian rule, high taxes, and state monopolies, which likely led to persecution and the murder of his son.

timeline.event: Meets Ibn Tumart and becomes his devoted follower and chief lieutenant.
Succeeds Ibn Tumart as leader of the Almohad movement after concealing his death to ensure a smooth transition of power.
Conquers Marrakesh, ending the Almoravid dynasty and establishing the city as the Almohad capital.
Conquers the central and eastern Maghreb (modern Algeria and Tunisia), unifying North Africa under his rule.
Crosses to Iberia to organize his dominions in Al-Andalus and prepare for further campaigns against Christian kingdoms.
Dies in Salé, Morocco, while assembling a massive army for a major invasion of Spain.

keyAchievements.title: Founding the Almohad Caliphate
keyAchievements.description: He transformed the Almohad religious movement into a powerful, institutionalized state and empire.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of the Maghreb
keyAchievements.description: He was the first ruler to unite the entire North African coast from the Atlantic to Libya under a single native rule.

keyAchievements.title: Military Victories
keyAchievements.description: He orchestrated the complete overthrow of the Almoravid dynasty and expanded the empire into Al-Andalus (Islamic Spain).

faq.question: Was Abd al-Mu'min related to Ibn Tumart?
faq.answer: No, he was not related by blood. He was a Berber from the Zenata confederation, while Ibn Tumart was from the Masmuda. His succession was based on his loyalty, skill, and being named the heir by Ibn Tumart.

faq.question: What does 'Almohad' mean?
faq.answer: It comes from the Arabic 'al-Muwahhidun,' which means 'the monotheists' or 'the unitarians.' It refers to their strict theological emphasis on the oneness of God (Tawhid).

faq.question: What was his major architectural legacy?
faq.answer: He commissioned the construction of the Koutoubia Mosque in Marrakesh, one of the most iconic examples of Almohad architecture, though it was largely completed by his successors.

timeline.event: Born near Mascara, in present-day Algeria, to a prominent religious and scholarly family.
Elected Emir by tribal leaders to organize and lead the resistance against the French colonial invasion.
Signs the Treaty of Tafna with France, which recognized his authority over a large portion of Algeria.
War resumes after France violates the treaty, leading to a brutal campaign of 'total war' by the French.
Surrenders to French forces to prevent the further annihilation of his people, on the condition of exile to Alexandria or Acre.
Imprisoned in France after the French government broke the terms of his surrender.
Released by order of Napoleon III and goes into exile, eventually settling in Damascus.
Intervenes to save thousands of Christians from massacres during sectarian riots in Damascus, earning international acclaim.
Dies in Damascus and is buried near the great Sufi master Ibn Arabi, a symbol of both resistance and compassion.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the Algerian State
keyAchievements.description: He united disparate tribes and established a modern state with its own army, currency, and educational system.

keyAchievements.title: Leader of Anti-Colonial Resistance
keyAchievements.description: For 15 years, he led a powerful and sophisticated military and political resistance against the French invasion.

keyAchievements.title: Humanitarian and Interfaith Icon
keyAchievements.description: He famously saved the lives of thousands of Christians in Damascus in 1860, embodying his principles of compassion.

faq.question: Why is he considered the 'father' of modern Algeria?
faq.answer: He was the first to unite various Algerian tribes under a single, organized state to resist foreign invasion, creating a powerful symbol of national identity that endures to this day.

faq.question: Was he only a warrior?
faq.answer: No, he was also a renowned Sufi theologian, poet, and philosopher who wrote extensively on spiritual matters and dedicated his later life to scholarship and teaching.

faq.question: What was his relationship with the French after his surrender?
faq.answer: Though he fought them fiercely, he earned their respect. After his imprisonment, he was honored by many in France, and his humanitarian actions in Damascus were celebrated across Europe and America.

timeline.event: Born near Bulawayo in Southern Rhodesia (now Zimbabwe).
Graduates from a teaching course at Adams College, becoming a teacher there.
Elected chief of the Abasemakholweni Zulu community in Groutville.
Joins the African National Congress (ANC).
Leads the Defiance Campaign and is deposed as chief by the apartheid government. He is elected President-General of the ANC later that year.
Arrested and charged with high treason in the Treason Trial, along with 155 others.
The Sharpeville Massacre occurs, leading to the banning of the ANC.
Awarded the 1960 Nobel Peace Prize for his non-violent struggle against apartheid.
Dies in a train accident near his home in Stanger (now KwaDukuza) while under a government ban.

keyAchievements.title: President-General of the ANC
keyAchievements.description: He led the African National Congress from 1952 until his death, guiding it through major non-violent resistance campaigns.

keyAchievements.title: 1960 Nobel Peace Prize
keyAchievements.description: Became the first African to be awarded the Nobel Peace Prize, bringing global attention to the anti-apartheid cause.

keyAchievements.title: Champion of Non-Violence
keyAchievements.description: Remained a steadfast advocate for non-violent resistance against the brutal apartheid regime, inspired by his Christian faith and Gandhian principles.

faq.question: Why was he called 'Chief Luthuli'?
faq.answer: He was the elected chief of his Zulu community in Groutville from 1936. The apartheid government deposed him in 1952 for his anti-apartheid activities, but he remained a respected leader to his people.

faq.question: Why did he win the Nobel Peace Prize?
faq.answer: He was awarded the prize for his leadership in the non-violent struggle against racial discrimination and apartheid in South Africa, which brought international focus to the injustice.

faq.question: Did he work with Nelson Mandela?
faq.answer: Yes, Luthuli was the President-General of the ANC during the time Nelson Mandela rose to prominence. They were colleagues in the struggle, though they sometimes had different tactical views as the movement evolved.

faq.question: Why did he insist on non-violence?
faq.answer: His commitment to non-violence was rooted in his deep Christian faith and his belief in the moral superiority and ultimate effectiveness of peaceful protest over armed conflict.

timeline.event: Born in Besease, Ashanti Empire.
Appointed Queen Mother of Ejisu by her brother, the Ejisuhene (chief).
King Prempeh I of the Ashanti Empire is deposed and exiled to the Seychelles by the British.
Governor Hodgson demands the Golden Stool, sparking Yaa Asantewaa's famous call to arms.
Leads the Ashanti forces as commander-in-chief in the War of the Golden Stool, besieging the British in Kumasi.
Captured by the British and exiled to the Seychelles.
Dies in exile in the Seychelles on October 17.

keyAchievements.title: Commander-in-Chief of the Ashanti Army
keyAchievements.description: She was appointed leader of the Ashanti army in the 1900 rebellion against the British, a rare role for a woman.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Anti-Colonial Resistance
keyAchievements.description: Her leadership and defiance made her a national hero in Ghana and an icon of resistance across Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Upholder of Ashanti Tradition
keyAchievements.description: She led a war to protect the Golden Stool, the sacred symbol of the Ashanti people's unity and soul, from desecration.

faq.question: What was the Golden Stool?
faq.answer: The Golden Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi) is the royal and divine throne of the Ashanti people. It is believed to house the spirit of the nation and is the ultimate symbol of power and unity.

faq.question: Was Yaa Asantewaa a king?
faq.answer: No, she was the Queen Mother (Ohemaa) of Ejisu, a powerful political and social role responsible for advising the chief and selecting his successor.

faq.question: Did the Ashanti win the war?
faq.answer: No, the Ashanti were defeated by the technologically superior British forces. The Ashanti Empire was annexed into the Gold Coast colony, but the Golden Stool was never captured by the British.

timeline.event: Born in Alexandria, Egypt, to a Christian family.
